content.text: A GenAI maturity model gives enterprises a shared language for describing where they are, where they want to be, and what the gaps are. Without it, maturity discussions degenerate into anecdote: 'we're further along than our peers' based on the number of POCs running rather than the depth and sustainability of the capability. A well-structured rubric converts that conversation into a diagnostic with specific, actionable gaps per dimension.
Maturity models serve three organisational purposes in a GenAI programme:
— Identify where the organisation is weakest before deciding where to invest. Spending on model fine-tuning when data quality is at Level 1 is wasted.
— Quarterly assessments against a stable rubric show whether interventions are working. Without a baseline, you cannot distinguish genuine progress from activity.
— A shared six-dimension model lets the CTO benchmark against industry peers without disclosing proprietary data, since the rubric is public and the scores are self-reported.

Maturity models are diagnostic instruments, not competition scorecards. Level 5 on every dimension is not the goal — the goal is the level that delivers the programme's strategic objectives at acceptable risk.
Measures whether AI investment is directed by a coherent strategy or driven by opportunistic experimentation:
No formal AI strategy; isolated POCs driven by individual curiosity
Informal AI strategy exists as a slide deck; not linked to business planning
Documented AI roadmap aligned to 2-3 business objectives; annual review cycle
AI roadmap integrated into corporate strategy; ESC governs portfolio; quarterly refresh
AI is a board-level strategic priority; competitive differentiation thesis defined and tracked
Measures the quality of the data estate and the maturity of the platform on which models run:
No data catalogue; data accessed ad hoc; no versioning or lineage
Data available for specific POCs; quality not systematically tracked; no feature store
Centralised data platform; basic quality metrics; model registry in place; CI/CD for models
Feature store with reuse across teams; automated data quality gates; full LLMOps pipeline
Real-time data flows; automated drift detection; data mesh with AI-ready contracts across BUs
Measures whether the organisation has the people, skills, and cultural norms to sustain AI delivery:
No dedicated AI staff; knowledge concentrated in 1-2 individuals
Small AI team (3-5); no formal upskilling; executives lack AI literacy
CoE established; AI guild network active; executive AI literacy programme underway
Embedded AI leads in each BU; structured career path for AI engineers; AI literacy mandatory for senior managers
AI capability woven into hiring, promotion, and performance criteria; external talent magnet reputation
Measures the robustness of controls over AI risks including bias, privacy, safety, and regulatory compliance:
No AI risk framework; risks managed informally or not at all
Basic data privacy controls; no bias testing; incident handling ad hoc
AI risk policy published; bias and fairness testing in deployment checklist; ESC owns risk oversight
Automated bias monitoring in production; red-teaming on Tier-1 systems; regulatory mapping complete
Proactive regulatory engagement; external AI ethics advisory board; zero-tolerance incident culture with rapid OODA loop
Measures the speed, reliability, and repeatability of getting AI systems into production and keeping them there:
No defined delivery process; models deployed manually by engineers
Basic deployment scripts; no monitoring; updates require full redeployment
CI/CD pipelines for model deployment; uptime SLAs defined; basic observability dashboards
Automated rollback on performance regression; shadow deployment for canary testing; P95 latency tracked per model
Multi-region active-active deployment; self-healing pipelines; sub-minute incident detection and isolation
Measures whether the organisation can demonstrate business value from AI investment and attribute it accurately:
No value metrics beyond technical outputs; ROI not tracked
Activity metrics reported (models trained, POCs completed); no business KPI linkage
Business KPIs defined for each use case; quarterly value reviews against investment case
Causal attribution methodology; A/B testing in production; portfolio ROI tracked quarterly by ESC
Real-time value dashboards; incremental ROI compared to alternatives; value used to fund next-generation R&D

The assessment rubric uses a structured interview process across five evidence categories for each dimension. Evidence is collected from three stakeholder groups: the CoE Lead and programme team, BU representatives, and an independent reviewer (internal audit or external assessor).
Policies, architectures, roadmaps, training records — existence and recency
Walk through how a specific process (deployment, risk review, data onboarding) actually works — not how it is described in a policy
Live demonstration of platforms, dashboards, and monitoring systems
Actual KPI data, incident logs, bias audit results — not projections
Independent interviews with BU leads and engineers who interact with the CoE daily

Scores are assigned as integers 1–5. Half-scores are not used during the assessment — if evidence is mixed between two levels, assign the lower level and note the gap. This prevents score inflation that makes benchmark comparisons meaningless.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Literal

Level = Literal[1, 2, 3, 4, 5]

LEVEL_LABELS = {
    1: "Exploring",
    2: "Experimenting",
    3: "Scaling",
    4: "Optimising",
    5: "Leading",
}

DIMENSIONS = [
    "strategy_vision",
    "data_infrastructure",
    "talent_culture",
    "risk_governance",
    "delivery_ops",
    "value_measurement",
]

@dataclass
class MaturityAssessment:
    organisation: str
    strategy_vision: Level
    data_infrastructure: Level
    talent_culture: Level
    risk_governance: Level
    delivery_ops: Level
    value_measurement: Level

    def overall(self) -> float:
        scores = [
            self.strategy_vision,
            self.data_infrastructure,
            self.talent_culture,
            self.risk_governance,
            self.delivery_ops,
            self.value_measurement,
        ]
        return sum(scores) / len(scores)

    def weakest_dimension(self) -> str:
        scores = {d: getattr(self, d) for d in DIMENSIONS}
        return min(scores, key=scores.get)

    def strongest_dimension(self) -> str:
        scores = {d: getattr(self, d) for d in DIMENSIONS}
        return max(scores, key=scores.get)

    def summary(self) -> str:
        avg = self.overall()
        label = LEVEL_LABELS[round(avg)]
        lines = [
            f"Maturity Assessment: {self.organisation}",
            f"  Overall: {avg:.1f} / 5.0 ({label})",
            f"  Strongest: {self.strongest_dimension()}",
            f"  Weakest: {self.weakest_dimension()}",
            "  Dimension scores:",
        ]
        for d in DIMENSIONS:
            score = getattr(self, d)
            lines.append(f"    {d}: {score} ({LEVEL_LABELS[score]})")
        return "\n".join(lines)

# Example assessment
assessment = MaturityAssessment(
    organisation="Acme Corp",
    strategy_vision=3,
    data_infrastructure=4,
    talent_culture=2,
    risk_governance=3,
    delivery_ops=4,
    value_measurement=2,
)
print(assessment.summary())
# Maturity Assessment: Acme Corp
#   Overall: 3.0 / 5.0 (Scaling)
#   Strongest: data_infrastructure
#   Weakest: talent_culture
#   ...

def prioritise_gaps(
    assessment: MaturityAssessment,
    target_level: int = 4,
) -> list[tuple[str, int, int]]:
    """
    Return (dimension, current_level, gap) sorted by gap descending.
    Only dimensions below target_level are returned.
    """
    gaps = []
    for d in DIMENSIONS:
        current = getattr(assessment, d)
        gap = target_level - current
        if gap > 0:
            gaps.append((d, current, gap))
    gaps.sort(key=lambda x: x[2], reverse=True)
    return gaps

gaps = prioritise_gaps(assessment, target_level=4)
print("Priority gaps to reach Level 4:")
for dim, current, gap in gaps:
    print(f"  {dim}: {current} -> 4 (gap = {gap})")
# Priority gaps to reach Level 4:
#   talent_culture: 2 -> 4 (gap = 2)
#   value_measurement: 2 -> 4 (gap = 2)
#   strategy_vision: 3 -> 4 (gap = 1)
#   risk_governance: 3 -> 4 (gap = 1)
A GenAI maturity assessment is not a one-time exercise. The recommended cadence depends on programme phase:
CoE Lead; independent validation by risk team
All 6 dimensions; peer benchmark if available
Focus on lagging dimensions + regulatory compliance

An assessment conducted only by the CoE team will drift toward optimism over time. Build in independent validation — at minimum, have internal audit verify the evidence for the two lowest-scoring dimensions.

Not all six dimensions carry equal weight for every organisation. A financial services firm regulated under DORA and the EU AI Act will weight Risk and Governance more heavily than a B2B SaaS company running customer-facing copilots. A weighted scoring model lets the assessment reflect strategic priorities:
Weights are agreed with the ESC before the baseline assessment and held constant across assessment cycles — changing weights between assessments makes trend comparison impossible.

def weighted_score(
    assessment,
    weights: dict[str, float],
) -> float:
    """Compute weighted overall maturity score."""
    total = sum(
        getattr(assessment, dim) * weight
        for dim, weight in weights.items()
    )
    return total

FS_WEIGHTS = {
    "strategy_vision": 0.15,
    "data_infrastructure": 0.20,
    "talent_culture": 0.10,
    "risk_governance": 0.30,
    "delivery_ops": 0.10,
    "value_measurement": 0.15,
}

# Using the assessment from the previous example
score = weighted_score(assessment, FS_WEIGHTS)
print(f"Weighted maturity score (FS variant): {score:.2f}")
# Weighted maturity score (FS variant): 3.00
# (strategy=3x0.15 + data=4x0.20 + talent=2x0.10 +
#  risk=3x0.30 + delivery=4x0.10 + value=2x0.15)
The assessment is conducted as a structured two-day workshop with three sessions. Preparation is as important as the sessions themselves:
Policy docs, platform screenshots, KPI data, incident logs
Final scores; gap prioritisation; 90-day action plan

The calibration session is where discrepancies between CoE self-assessment and BU stakeholder feedback are resolved. The rule is: where the two views diverge by more than one level, the lower score stands until the discrepancy is explained by documented evidence.
For each dimension, the PMO should collect the following before the workshop:
— AI roadmap document, ESC minutes from last two quarters, board presentation with AI content.
— Data catalogue screenshots, MLOps pipeline diagram, model registry export, last month's data quality report.
— Org chart with AI roles, training completion records, job descriptions for AI engineer and ML engineer roles.
— AI risk policy document, last bias audit report, incident log for past 12 months, regulatory mapping spreadsheet.
— CI/CD pipeline diagram, uptime SLA reports, deployment frequency metrics, on-call runbook.
— KPI dashboard screenshots, investment case vs. actuals comparison, last quarterly value review report.

Missing evidence is itself a scoring signal. If the PMO cannot locate a data quality report, the team does not have one — score Data & Infrastructure accordingly, do not accept a verbal assurance that 'it exists somewhere'.
Several external benchmarks exist against which to contextualise internal assessment scores. Key sources published as of 2025:
McKinsey Global AI Survey (annual): company-size-stratified AI adoption and value metrics.
Gartner AI Maturity Model: five-level model with similar dimension structure, useful for cross-vendor comparison.
BCG AI Advantage Index: industry-specific benchmarks for financial services, healthcare, and manufacturing.
O'Reilly AI Adoption in the Enterprise (annual): practitioner-reported data on tooling, skills, and deployment patterns.

External benchmarks calibrate ambition: a Dimension 2 score in an industry where the median is 2.5 is not a crisis; the same score in an industry where leading firms are at 4 signals a material competitive gap. Do not set improvement targets in isolation from the competitive context.
— Teams rate where they plan to be in 6 months, not where they are today. Enforce the rule: score against current observable evidence only.
— Risk and Governance scores get inflated because the data platform is strong. Each dimension must be scored independently.
— The CoE Lead's self-assessment systematically overscores; BU feedback systematically underscores. Triangulate across both.
— Programmes focus investment on dimensions already at Level 3+ because that is where the team is comfortable. The weakest dimension is almost always the binding constraint.
— Dimensions and level descriptors should be reviewed annually as the market evolves. What was Level 5 in 2023 may be Level 3 by 2026.

The GenAI maturity model has six dimensions: Strategy & Vision, Data & Infrastructure, Talent & Culture, Risk & Governance, Delivery & Ops, and Value & Measurement — each scored 1 (Exploring) to 5 (Leading).
Scores are assigned based on observable evidence in five categories: documented artefacts, process execution, tool state, outcome data, and stakeholder testimony. Half-scores are not permitted.
The assessment is not a one-time exercise — baseline before programme start, semi-annual in Pilot, annual from Scale onwards, with independent validation of lagging dimensions.
Gap prioritisation (target level minus current level) drives investment sequencing: the highest-gap dimension is almost always the binding constraint on overall programme performance.
Common pitfalls — scoring to aspiration, single-respondent bias, ignoring the weakest dimension — are governance design problems fixed by independent validation and evidence-only scoring rules.
